theme: Distinct enemy behaviors and spawn tables
summary: Populate the dungeon with monsters that act on their own turns, fight the player, and exhibit at least four distinct AI behaviors (chaser, ambusher, ranged, fleeing-when-hurt). Each floor draws monsters from a seedable spawn table; monster turns are driven by FOV-based aggro using the field-of-view introduced in Sprint 3; combat resolves through the same bump-to-attack and damage system Sprint 3 established. Items dropped or carried by monsters integrate with Sprint 4's inventory. Monster behavior must be deterministic given the dungeon seed so runs remain reproducible.
assumptions: Sprints 3 (FOV/Combat/Stats) and 4 (Items/Inventory) have been merged before Sprint 5 begins. If they are not, Sprint 5 must add minimum stubs (HP/attack/defense, FOV computation, inventory) sufficient to satisfy this contract; in that case those stubs must be production-quality and pass their own Sprint 3/4 invariants.
Sprint 3 introduced an Entity hierarchy with HP/attack/defense fields and a damage-resolution function; monsters inherit from the same Entity base as Player.
Sprint 3 produced a `compute_fov(floor, origin, radius)` function (or equivalent) returning the set/grid of tiles visible from an origin. Sprint 5 uses it for monster aggro.
Sprint 2's seedable World/Floor abstraction is intact; spawn tables draw from a per-floor RNG derived from the master seed.
Bump-to-attack already works for the player attacking entities; Sprint 5 extends the same primitive to monsters attacking the player.
The game's main loop runs the player's action, then iterates over each living monster on the current floor and invokes a `take_turn(game)` (or equivalent).

Glyph legend extends Sprint 2's: monsters render as letters (lowercase or uppercase per archetype). The exact letters are an implementation choice, but the renderer must distinguish monster glyphs from '#', '.', '+', '<', '>', '@'.
Pytest remains the test runner; tests live under `tests/`.

criteria.description: A dedicated monsters module exists (e.g., `whisperdeep/monsters.py` or `whisperdeep/monster/`) and is importable.
testSteps: Locate a Python module whose name contains 'monster' under `whisperdeep/`.
Run `python -c "import whisperdeep.monsters"` (or the discovered name) and confirm exit code 0.
criteria.threshold: Module file exists and imports cleanly.

criteria.description: A dedicated AI / behavior module exists (e.g., `whisperdeep/ai.py`) housing the behavior implementations, separate from monster data definitions.
testSteps: Locate a Python module under `whisperdeep/` whose primary contents are AI/behavior classes or functions (filename or module docstring references AI or behavior).
Confirm it imports cleanly.
criteria.threshold: AI/behavior module is present and distinct from the monster-data module.

criteria.description: Monster is an Entity subclass (or composes with Entity) carrying at minimum: position (x, y), glyph, name, hp/max_hp, attack, defense, and a behavior reference.
testSteps: Instantiate a monster via the public API.
Inspect attribute presence: x, y, glyph, name, hp, max_hp, attack, defense, and either `behavior`, `ai`, or `take_turn`.
criteria.threshold: All listed attributes exist and have sensible default types (ints for stats, str for name/glyph).

criteria.description: At least 5 distinct monster definitions are registered (e.g., rat, goblin, archer, mushroom-spawner, wraith). Each has a unique name and glyph.
testSteps: Enumerate the registered monster definitions/templates.
Verify there are at least 5.
Verify all names are unique and all glyphs are unique (or, if glyphs may collide, verify each glyph maps to a single canonical monster type).
criteria.threshold: >=5 monster definitions; unique names; unique glyphs (or documented intentional collisions).

criteria.description: At least 4 distinct AI behaviors are implemented and selectable: chaser (moves toward player when seen), ambusher (waits until player is adjacent or close, then attacks), ranged (attacks from distance without closing), and fleeing-when-hurt (retreats below an HP threshold).
testSteps: Enumerate the AI behavior classes/functions exported by the AI module.
Verify there are at least 4 distinct behaviors with names mapping to: chaser, ambusher, ranged, fleeing-when-hurt (or close synonyms documented in the README).
For each behavior, instantiate a monster using it and confirm the behavior reference is set.
criteria.threshold: At least 4 distinct behavior implementations exist and are wired to monster definitions.

criteria.description: Chaser behavior: when the player is within the monster's FOV, the monster moves one tile closer to the player along a sensible path; when the player is not in FOV, the monster does not chase.
testSteps: Build a small floor with a known room and corridor.
Place a chaser monster at position M and the player at position P, with a clear line-of-sight at distance 5.
Run one monster turn.
Verify the Chebyshev or Manhattan distance from monster to player decreases by exactly 1 (or the monster attacks if adjacent).
Repeat with the player placed outside the monster's FOV (e.g., behind a wall) and verify the monster's position is unchanged or follows an idle policy (no movement toward the player).
criteria.threshold: With LOS: distance to player decreases by 1 per turn until adjacent. Without LOS: monster does not move toward the player.

criteria.description: Ranged behavior: when the player is within FOV and within the monster's attack range (>1 tile), the monster attacks at distance and the player loses HP without the monster moving adjacent.
testSteps: Place a ranged monster and the player with clear LOS at distance 4.
Note player HP.
Run one monster turn.

Verify either (a) player HP decreased and the monster did not move into melee range, or (b) the monster moved at most 1 tile to gain a clean shot then attacked next turn — the contract requires that within 2 turns at least one ranged attack landed without the monster ever becoming adjacent.
criteria.threshold: Ranged attack lands within 2 monster turns without the monster ever entering an adjacent tile to the player.

criteria.description: Fleeing-when-hurt behavior: when the monster's HP drops below a documented threshold (e.g., <=25% of max_hp), it moves away from the player on subsequent turns instead of attacking.
testSteps: Place a fleeing-capable monster with HP set to <=25% of max_hp adjacent or within FOV of the player.
Run one monster turn.

Verify the distance from monster to player increases by 1 (or the monster moves into a tile farther from the player along walkable terrain). If no retreat tile is walkable, the monster may stand still — this fallback must be documented.
criteria.threshold: When HP <= flee_threshold, monster's distance to player strictly increases unless no retreat is walkable.

criteria.description: Ambusher behavior: monster does not move while outside a documented trigger range and the player is not adjacent; once the player enters trigger range OR an adjacent tile, the ambusher acts (chases or attacks).
testSteps: Place an ambusher and the player at distance > trigger_range with LOS.
Run 3 monster turns and verify the ambusher's position is unchanged.
Move the player to within trigger_range or adjacency.
Run 1-2 more monster turns and verify the ambusher now moves toward or attacks the player.
criteria.threshold: Ambusher idles outside trigger range; activates inside trigger range.

criteria.description: FOV-based aggro: monsters use a field-of-view check (line-of-sight + sight radius) to decide whether the player is 'seen'. A monster behind a wall does not act on the player.
testSteps: Construct a floor with a wall separating the monster from the player.
Place a chaser monster on one side and the player on the other.
Run 3 monster turns.
Verify the chaser's position has not moved toward the player (because LOS is blocked).
criteria.threshold: Monster does not advance through walls or react to a player it cannot see.

criteria.description: Spawn tables: each floor has a documented spawn table mapping monster types to relative weights, and the generator selects monsters by weighted draw from a seeded RNG.
testSteps: Locate the spawn-table data structure (e.g., a dict, JSON file, or Python literal).
Verify it lists at least 3 monster types with weights.
Generate two floors with the same seed and dimensions and confirm the resulting monster lists are identical (composition + positions).
Generate two floors with different seeds and confirm at least one differs in monster composition or positions.
criteria.threshold: Spawn tables exist; same-seed -> identical monsters; different seeds -> divergent monsters.

criteria.description: Each floor spawns at least 1 monster and at most a documented cap (e.g., <=20) at generation time; monsters are placed on walkable, non-stair tiles inside rooms (not corridors-only, not on the player's spawn).
testSteps: Generate 3 floors with different seeds at the standard size.
For each floor, count monsters and verify count >= 1 and <= the documented cap.
For each monster, verify its tile is walkable, is not '<' or '>', and is not the player's spawn tile.
criteria.threshold: Every floor has between 1 and <cap> monsters placed legally.

criteria.description: Monster turn order: after the player acts, every living monster on the current floor takes exactly one turn before control returns to the player.
testSteps: Construct a game with 3 chaser monsters on the current floor positioned away from the player but with LOS.
Snapshot each monster's position.
Issue a single player action (e.g., wait or move).
Verify each monster's position has updated (or it attacked) once and the turn counter advanced by 1.
criteria.threshold: All living monsters acted exactly once per player turn.

criteria.description: Bump-to-attack works in both directions: the player bumping into a monster damages it; a monster moving into the player's tile damages the player.
testSteps: Place the player adjacent to a monster with known stats.
Issue a player move into the monster's tile and verify monster HP decreased and player did not move onto the monster's tile.
Reset; place the monster adjacent to the player with a chaser behavior; run one monster turn; verify player HP decreased.
criteria.threshold: Both directions of bump-to-attack reduce the defender's HP; neither attacker moves onto the defender's tile.

criteria.description: Monster death: when a monster's HP reaches 0 or below, it is removed from the floor's entity list and no longer takes turns or blocks movement.
testSteps: Place a monster with HP=1 adjacent to the player.
Have the player attack it.
Verify it is no longer in the floor's living-entities collection.
Verify the player can move onto its former tile.
Run a monster turn step and verify the dead monster does not act.
criteria.threshold: Dead monsters are removed; their tile becomes walkable; they take no further turns.

criteria.description: Player death: if a monster reduces the player's HP to <=0, the game enters a death state (game-over flag set) and stops accepting move actions. Full save/load and chronicle are out of scope (Sprint 6/10), but a death flag and a halt to actions are required here.
testSteps: Set the player's HP low enough to die in one hit.
Place an adjacent attacking monster and run a monster turn.
Verify the game exposes a `game_over` (or equivalent) attribute that is True.
Issue a player move and verify it is rejected or a no-op.
criteria.threshold: Game-over flag becomes True on player death; subsequent player actions do not move the player.

criteria.description: Monster behaviors are deterministic given the master seed: replaying the same sequence of player actions on the same seed produces the same monster movements, attacks, and HP outcomes.
testSteps: Construct two games with master seed=12345 and identical initial conditions.
Apply the same scripted sequence of player actions to both (e.g., 10 moves and waits).
After each action, snapshot every monster's (x, y, hp).
Compare snapshots step-by-step.
criteria.threshold: Snapshots match 100% across the two runs.

criteria.description: Monsters are rendered with their own glyphs on the current floor; only monsters within the player's FOV are drawn (fog-of-war hides unseen monsters).
testSteps: Generate a frame with a monster in the player's FOV; verify the monster's glyph appears at its tile.
Move the monster (or place a second one) outside the player's FOV; verify the rendered frame does not show that monster's glyph at its tile.
criteria.threshold: In-FOV monsters render; out-of-FOV monsters do not.

criteria.description: Monster glyphs do not collide with terrain glyphs ('#', '.', '+', '<', '>', '@'). They use distinct printable characters (e.g., letters).
testSteps: Enumerate all registered monster definitions and collect their glyphs.
Verify none equal '#', '.', '+', '<', '>', or '@'.
criteria.threshold: Zero glyph collisions with the reserved terrain/player set.

criteria.description: The existing `--seed` flag, when combined with monster spawning, still produces fully reproducible runs end-to-end (dungeon + monsters).
testSteps: Run `python -m whisperdeep --seed 7 --headless` (or equivalent snapshot mode) and capture an initial state including monster positions.
Run again with `--seed 7` and capture again.
Verify both initial monster lists (positions + types) are identical.
Run with `--seed 8` and verify the monster list differs from `--seed 7`.
criteria.threshold: Same seed -> identical monster placement; different seed -> different monster placement.

criteria.description: Automated test suite includes >=8 tests targeting monsters and AI: at minimum one for each of the 4 behaviors, one for FOV-based aggro, one for spawn-table determinism, one for monster-turn ordering, and one for combat death.
testSteps: Locate test files under `tests/` whose names reference monster or AI.
Run `pytest tests/` and verify all tests pass.
Count tests in the monster/AI test files and verify >=8.
criteria.threshold: All tests pass; >=8 tests cover the listed scenarios.

criteria.description: Sprints 1, 2, 3, and 4 functionality is preserved: the game still launches, the dungeon still generates, FOV/combat still work, and items can still be picked up, equipped, and used. All prior tests still pass.
testSteps: Run `pytest tests/` against the full repository.
Launch `python -m whisperdeep --seed 1` (interactive or headless) and verify it starts without exceptions.
Verify a player movement command still works on an empty corridor (Sprint 1/2 regression).
Verify FOV is still computed and fog-of-war still hides unseen tiles (Sprint 3 regression).
Verify the player can still pick up an item placed in their tile (Sprint 4 regression).
criteria.threshold: All existing tests pass; game launches; movement, FOV, and item pickup all still function.

criteria.description: Documentation (README or docs/) describes the monster system: how spawn tables work, the implemented AI behaviors and their rules, the glyph legend addition for monsters, and any tunable parameters (sight radius, flee threshold, ambusher trigger range).
testSteps: Open README.md or docs/ files and search for sections referencing 'monster', 'AI' or 'behavior', and 'spawn'.
Verify each of the 4 behaviors is documented by name and rule.
Verify the monster glyph legend (or rule for assigning glyphs) is documented.
Verify at least one tunable parameter (sight radius / flee threshold / trigger range) is documented.
criteria.threshold: All four documentation topics are present.

outOfScope: Permadeath save/load, run-summary screen, daily seeds (Sprint 6).
The Whisperer LLM adapter, event bus, prose pool (Sprint 7).
Whisperer panel UI, monster naming on first sight (Sprint 8) — Sprint 5 monsters have static names; the Whisperer's first-sight renaming is Sprint 8.
Per-archetype monster pools and palettes (Sprint 11) — Sprint 5 uses a single global spawn table or a per-floor table parameterized only by depth.
Status effects from monster attacks (poison, burning, slowed). Sprint 5 monster attacks may apply only flat damage; status-effect application is acceptable but not required.
Pathfinding beyond simple greedy step-toward / step-away. A* and corridor-aware navigation are nice-to-have, not contractual.
Monster behavior trees with composite nodes — simple state machines or single-function behaviors are sufficient.
Ranged projectile rendering as moving glyphs across multiple frames; an instant ranged hit with a log entry suffices.
Summoner / boss / unique monsters — these can be designed in but are not required by this contract.
deliverables: Monsters module with >=5 monster definitions, each with stats, glyph, name, and a behavior reference.
AI/behavior module implementing >=4 distinct behaviors (chaser, ambusher, ranged, fleeing-when-hurt).
Spawn-table data + integration with the floor generator so monsters are placed deterministically per seed.
Game-loop wiring so monsters take exactly one turn per player turn, with FOV-based aggro and bump-to-attack in both directions.
Renderer extension to draw monsters within the player's FOV using non-colliding glyphs.
`game_over` (or equivalent) flag set on player death, halting further player actions.
Automated tests (>=8) covering each behavior, FOV aggro, spawn determinism, turn order, and combat death.
Updated documentation: monster system, AI behaviors, spawn tables, glyph legend additions, tunable parameters.
